topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. It was sterile, professional, and utterly devastating. The headline, the byline—it was all wrong. My meticulously crafted piece, the one I’d poured six months of sleepless nights into, was attributed to a stranger. A stranger who had apparently stolen my voice, my perspective, my entire narrative. I stared at the screen, the fluorescent office light buzzing overhead seeming to amplify the silence in the room. My hands, usually steady when typing, began to tremble so violently I had to grip the edge of my desk to steady them. A hot, prickly sensation bloomed behind my eyes, and I could taste metal in my mouth. I didn't move for a long time, just letting the weight of the deception settle, a crushing pressure behind my ribs. When I finally stood up, the floor seemed to tilt slightly. Every breath felt shallow, as if the air itself had been withdrawn.

[story 2]

I reread the publication notice three times, the words blurring into meaningless shapes. It wasn't just a mistake; it was a complete erasure. The realization hit me with a sudden, cold clarity that stole the air from my lungs. I sank into the worn armchair, the fabric rough against my skin, and curled inward, pulling my knees tight to my chest. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a choked whisper that caught in my chest. My vision tunneled, the edges darkening as a profound emptiness washed over me. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically scrub the sight away. The room spun, a dizzying kaleidoscope of meaningless shapes. I felt utterly hollowed out, as if the core of me had been surgically removed, leaving behind only a cold, echoing space where passion used to reside.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, the world narrowed instantly to the stark white text. My jaw locked so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I slowly lowered the phone, the glass cool against my fingertips. There was no anger yet, only a vast, suffocating quiet spreading through my chest. I walked to the window, pulling the heavy curtain aside just enough to see the indifferent city below. The noise of traffic sounded distant, muffled, as if I were underwater. A single tear escaped, tracing a slow, hot path down my cheek. It wasn't a dramatic weeping; it was a quiet, relentless seepage, a physical manifestation of the sheer imbalance that had occurred. I stood there, motionless, feeling profoundly adrift, like a ship that had suddenly lost its anchor in a raging, uncaring sea.
